era_context: The mid-14th century in Europe is marked by the aftermath of the Black Death and the emergence of new social and economic structures. The Hundred Years' War between England and France continues, with England in a phase of military dominance. In Asia, the Yuan Dynasty is in decline, and the Ming Dynasty is consolidating power (formally established 1368). The Ottoman Empire is expanding in Anatolia and the Balkans. The papacy remains in Avignon, though reform movements are gaining momentum. This is an era of religious ferment, early humanism, and the transformation of feudal society.

events.title: Battle of Auray; Breton Succession War concludes
events.description: The Battle of Auray on September 29, 1364, marks the conclusion of the Breton War of Succession (1341–1364). John of Montfort, supported by English forces, defeats Charles of Blois, supported by French forces. This victory secures John's control of Brittany and establishes it as a semi-independent duchy aligned with England. The battle is significant for its demonstration of English military superiority and for its role in the broader Hundred Years' War, as Brittany becomes a key English ally and base of operations.
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Auray is well-documented in multiple contemporary and near-contemporary chronicles. The date (September 29, 1364) is secure.

events.title: Zhu Yuanzhang (Hongwu Emperor) consolidates Ming Dynasty control
events.description: Zhu Yuanzhang, the founder of the Ming Dynasty, continues consolidating power in 1364. By this year, he has established his capital at Nanjing and is systematically eliminating rival warlords and consolidating control over China proper. In 1364, he is in the process of eliminating the last major rival, Zhang Shicheng, whose defeat in 1367 will complete Ming unification. This period sees the establishment of the Ming administrative system and the beginning of the dynasty's long reign (1368–1644).
key_figures: Zhu Yuanzhang (Hongwu Emperor, r. 1368–1398, consolidating power in 1364)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: Ming Dynasty consolidation is well-documented in official records. The year 1364 falls within the period of Zhu Yuanzhang's rise to power.

events.title: Ottoman Empire expands; Murad I consolidates Balkan territories
events.description: Murad I, the Ottoman Sultan, continues expanding Ottoman control in the Balkans in 1364. By this year, Ottoman forces have secured much of Thrace and are pressing toward major Byzantine and Serbian territories. This expansion marks the beginning of Ottoman dominance in southeastern Europe and the decline of Byzantine and Serbian power. The Ottoman expansion in the 1360s is part of a broader pattern of Ottoman military success that will continue for centuries.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the 1360s is documented in later Ottoman and Byzantine sources. The exact chronology of individual campaigns is sometimes uncertain.

events.title: Clement VI dies; Urban V becomes Pope
events.description: Pope Urban V (Guillaume de Grimoard) is elected Pope in 1362 and continues his papacy in 1364. Urban V is notable for his attempts to reform the Church and to return the papacy to Rome (which he achieves in 1367, though the return is temporary). In 1364, Urban V is consolidating his authority and pursuing his reform agenda. His papacy represents an attempt to address the institutional problems of the Church that will eventually lead to the Western Schism.
events.certainty_note: Urban V's papacy is well-documented in papal records. The year 1364 falls within his reign (1362–1370).

events.title: Tamerlane (Timur) rises to prominence in Transoxiana
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane) is rising to prominence in Transoxiana in the early-to-mid 1360s. By 1364, he has begun to establish himself as a military leader and is in the process of consolidating power in the region. His rise is marked by military victories and the elimination of rival khans. By the late 1360s, he will have established himself as the dominant power in Transoxiana and will begin his vast conquests across Asia and the Middle East.

sources.name: Ibn Arabshah, Aja'ib al-Maqdur fi Nawa'ib Timur

events.certainty_note: Timur's rise in the 1360s is documented in later Persian sources, but the exact chronology is uncertain. 1364 is approximate for his prominence at this time.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Auray (1364-001) is sometimes presented as a decisive victory that secured Breton independence, but modern scholarship emphasizes that Brittany remained contested and vulnerable to French pressure throughout the late 14th century. The characterization of Ming consolidation (1364-002) as a smooth process is problematic—Zhu Yuanzhang faced significant resistance and the consolidation was violent and contested. The Ottoman expansion narrative (1364-003) can be read as teleological, presenting Ottoman growth as inevitable when contemporary sources do not always emphasize Ottoman dominance. Urban V's papacy (1364-004) is sometimes presented as a reform success, but modern scholarship notes that his reforms were limited and that the underlying institutional problems persisted.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1364 is moderate but heavily weighted toward Europe and the Mediterranean. Western chronicles (Froissart, Jean de Venette) provide rich detail on the Hundred Years' War and Breton succession but are often biased toward court perspectives. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are detailed but reflect official historiography. Ottoman and Byzantine sources are later compilations, introducing chronological uncertainty. Persian sources on Timur are later compilations. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are largely absent from surviving written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Auray concludes the Breton War of Succession that began in 1341
Zhu Yuanzhang's consolidation in 1364 leads to the formal establishment of the Ming Dynasty in 1368
Urban V's papacy in 1364 leads to his return of the papacy to Rome in 1367
Timur's rise in 1364 leads to his defeat of Amir Husayn in 1365
